California
Fair Political
Practices Cornmlsslon
Deborah A. Churton
counsel, East PaIo AIto
Redevelopment Agency
ware and Freidenrich
400 Hanilton Avenue
PaIo AIto, CA 94301-1825
July 25, 1991
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
our Pile No. r -91-3 21
Dear Ms. churton:
This is in response to your letter requesting assistance on
behalf of East PaIo Alto Planning Commissioners MeIvin Harris,
Foster curry, Joseph Goodwill and Tony Franco concerning their
duties undei the conf I ict-o f - interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").1 
Since your request concerns a series of
decisions involved in the establishnent of a redevelopment project
area, ue are providing ygu Irith general guidelines applicable to
the decisions- generaliy.2 Please note that nothing in this letter
should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken
place.
OUESTIONS
1. May East PaIo Alto Planning Commissioners participate in
decisions concerning the Gateway RedeveloPnent Project if the
decisions nay directly and indirectly affect their real property
i nterests ?
1 co,r..rrr"nt code Sections 81ooo-9Lo15. AII statutory references
are to the Governnent code unless otherr./i.se indicated. 
commission
reguJ.ations appear at 2 california code of Regulations Section
180oo, et seo. Al1 references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 5 of the California code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
inmunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regul,ation 18329 (c) (3). )
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-O8OZ . (916) 322-5660

Page 2
2. wilI the decisions on the redevelopment project affect
the public Aenerally in the sane Banner as the pJ.anning
connissioners pursuant to the exceptions in the Act?
3. If a rnajority of planning cornmissioners are disqualified
due to financiaL interests which will be affected by the decision,
tnay sorne of the officials 
be perroitted to participate throughout
the series of decisions concerning the redevelopment area?
CONCLUS IONS
1. East PaIo AIto Planning commissioners nay not participate
in any decisions which r.riII have a foreseeable and material affect
on any of their econonic interests.
2. The ttpublic generally'r exception vill not apply unless
the decisions wiII affect a significant segment of East Palo Alto
in the sane nanner as the decisions will affect the interests of
the planning conmissioners. This does not appear to be the case
here .
3. Where a quorun cannot be achieved due to
disqualifications, 
a disqualified planning commissioner nay be
tegitty required to participate despite such disqualification. 
A
planning co:nnissioner may participate if he or she is selected at
iandon io achieve a quorum. If necessary, more than one planning
conmissioner may be selected at random to achieve the quorum.
once selected, the same pJ.anning conmissioner or
conmissioners may particiPate in all interrelated decisions.
other disqualified planning commissioners remain disqualified.
Should the commissioner chosen be unable to participate for sone
other reason, other planning conmissioners may not be substituted
for the cornmissioner chosen randomly to achieve the quorum. If
the subject matter of the decision is unchanged as are the
disqualifying interests, there is no basis to repeat the randon
selection process.
FACTS
The city of East Palo AIto is currently in the process of
irnplementing a series of redevelopment projects to increase the
city's tax base and enhance the city's property values. East PaIo
Alto covers two and one-ha1f square miles and has a population of
approximateLy 23,500 persons. Aside from a few retail
establishnents, the city is prirnarily a residential connunity
conprised of single and multi-fanily residentiaL units.
The Gateway Area is intended to be developed into a fuII-
scaIe, super-regional retail maIl (the I'project"). 
The project
area covers approxirnately L15 acres and is currently zoned for
single and nulti-family residential and some commercial uses. The
najor goal of the project would be to establish the cater,ray Area
as the retail center of the comrnunity.


Our File No. I-9■
-32■
Page 3
You stated that the planning conmission will be involved in a
series of decisions concerning the redeveloprnent project in the
near future, including the review of an environmental irnpact
report, general plan anendrnent, specific plan, rezoning amendment,
redevelopnent p1an, and, eventually, a planned use developnent
pernit application, tentative tract map and development agreement.
You have become concerned that because some of your planning
commissioners have property and business interests in and near the
project area, they may have conflicts of interest with respect to
the redevelopment proj ect.
You stated that Planning conmissioner Harris owns two single
faniJ.y residences within approximately 3oo feet of the project
area. one serves as his principal. residence, and the other is
Ieased out. 
Commissioner curry owns a single family residence
serving as his principal residence within 3oo feet of the project
area. In addition, during the Iast 12 months, Conmissioner curry
has received Sgoo in exchange for his nanagenent of rental
property located within the project area. The source of the
i.,"tr" 6,rnt p.op.tty in the project area- commissioner GoodwiII
owns four piices of commercial Property within l,0oo to 1,5oo feet
of the proJect area and his daughter, his dependent child, ovns a
piece of cornrnercial property also within 1,000 to 1,500 feet of
the project area. Commissioner Goodwill and his daughter lease
the par-els to tenants for retaiJ'/comnercia.l' uses- while the
project anticipates retail use, you stated that the type of retail
Lusine=="s curiently planned woul'd not compete with existing
sma1l.-scale conmercial and retail uses in the city. 
FinalIy,
commj-ssioner Franco oi{ns and operates a security alarm
instalLation business whj.ch is situated on property he leases in
the project area. some of his custoroers olrn comnercial and
residential property within the project area.
ANALYSIS
The Political Reforn Act (the trActrr), was enacted by the
people of the State of California by initiative 
in 1974' The
purpose for the conf I ict-o f- interest provisions of the Act was to
ensure that public officials, 
whether elected or appointed, would
perf orrn their duties in an impartial manner, free fron bias caused
by their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
In furtherance of this goal, section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any publ j,c officiat 
frorn naking, participating in
naking, or otherwise using his or her official 
position to
influence a governnental decision in which the official 
has a
financial interest. 
A "public official" 
is defined in Section
82048 and Regulation L8700 as every natural person who is a
menber, officer, 
ernployee, or consultant of a state or local
government agency. This definition r.rould include nernbers of the
East PaIo Alto Planning Comnission.


Our File No. I-9■
-32■
Page 4
1. Economic Interests
Section 87103 specifies that an official 
has a financial
interest within the neaning of Section 8710o if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a Daterial financial
effect, distinguishable fron the effect on the public generally,
on the official 
or a roenber of his or her inmediate family or on:
(a) Any business entity in nhich the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investnent lrorth
one thousand dollars ($1,ooo1 or more.
(b) Any real property in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars (S1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of incone, other than gifts
and other than loans by a connercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terns available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating tlro hundred fifty
dollars (S25o) or nore in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 nonths prior to the time lrhen the
decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, Partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any Position of management.
section 87103 (a) - (d) .
According to your facts, Planning comrnissioners Harris,
currv, coodwill and Pranco all have a variety of econonic
inteiests3 which may be foreseeably and rnaterially affected by the
redevelopment decisions.
The interests of the planning commissioners are as fol1or"s:
1. Planning Comnissioner Harris
A. A single family residence within 300 feet.
B. A single fanily residence vithin 3oo feet which is leased
out .
c. 
The lessee is also a source of income.
3 Pl.u"" be aware that $/here any one econonic interest of an
official 
wiII be foreseeably and naterially affected by a
decision, the official 
may not participate in the decision.


Our File No. 
■-9■-32■
Page 5
2. commissioner curry
A. A single fanily residence within 300 feet.
c. A source of incone owni.ng property in the proiect area.
3. Conmissi.oner Goodsi 1I
A. Four commercial properties within 1,000 to 1,500 feet-
B. An additional conmercial property within 1,000 to 1,500
feet owned by his dependent child.
c. Five retait-business lessees (sources of income)
operating businesses on the property within 1,0oo to 1,500 feet'
4. Commissioner Franco
A. A Leasehold interest in the project area.
B. A security alarm business in the project area.
c. 
customers in the Project area.
Thus, each planning connissioner is required to disqualify
hinself from making or participating in any decision which would
have a foreseeabl,e and material financial effect on the official's
economic interest that is distinguishable from the effect on the
public generallY.
2. 
Foreseeabi litv
whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably f oreseeabl,e at the tine a governnental decision is rnade
depends oi th" facts of each particular case. An effect is
co'nsidered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
Iikelihood that j.t lrilI occur. Certainty-is not required'
Ho"e"er, if an effect is only a nere possibility, 
it is.not
reasona;Iy foreseeable. (Downev cares v. Downev community
Develooment Cornrnission (1987) 196 Cal-App.3d 938; In re Thorner
(1975) 1 FPPC ops. 198.)
The anticipated result of any redeveloprnent plan is to
increase the property values and inprove the business clinate
within the project area. The very nature of redevelopnent
projects has led the Cornrnission to find that j't is reasonably
foreseeable that there will be a financial effect on real ProPerty
values and business interests located within or near project
areas. (In re Oqelsbv (1975) 1 FPPC OPS. 71; Armento Advice
Letter, No. A-90-499.) Since each planning commissioner has
economic interests in or near the project area, we Presume that a
financial effect on each is foreseeable.


Our File No. 1-9■
-32■
Page 6
3. Uaterial itv
However, disqual if ication is only required vhere the
foreseeabl.e effect on a planning cornrnissioner's econornic interest
is also material. 
The Commission has adopted a series of
regulations which provide guidance concerning whether the
foreseeable financial effects of a decision are material.
(Regulation L8702. ) These regulations apply different standards
depending on whether the decision wiII directly or indirectly
affect the official's 
econo:nic interest. 
Thus, we must consider
each interest of the planning conmissioners individually to
determine if disquatification is required.
A. Real Propertv
Planning commissioner Franco Ieases property in the
redevelopment area for hj-s alarn business. Both Planning
Comnissioners Harris and Curry own real property within 3o0 feet
of the project area. Planning conmissioner Goodwill o$/ns property
within L,OOO to 1,5o0 feet of the project area which he leases to
commerci-a1 businesses.
If Planning Commissioner Franco's Ieasehold interest in the
redevelopment aiea is for a term greater than month to month, it
is considered a real property interest under the Act. 
(Section
a2o33; Regulation 18233.) Regulation 18702.r(a) (3)(D) provides
that a decision's effect on an economic interest is deerned to be
rnaterial and disqualification is required if:
(D) The decision is to designate the survey
area, to select the project area, to adopt the
preliminary plan, to forrn a project area conmittee,
to certify the environlrental document, to adopt the
redevelopment p1an, to add territory to the
redevelop:nent area, or to rescind or arnend any of
the above decisions,' and real DroDertv in r./hich the
of f icial- has an interest' or anv part of it is
Located within the boundaries (or the oroposed
boundaries) of the rcdel{elapuen! a 
.
Regulation L8702.1(a) (3) (D),
emphasis added.
Thus, the decision to establish a redevelopment area that
encompasses the property Planning commissioner Franco leases is
deemed to have a material financial effect on his real property
and Planning Commissioner Franco may not participate.
Regulation L8702.3 (a) (1) provides the effect of a decision on
the real property interest of a public official 
which is wj.thin


Our File No. 1-91-32■
Page 7
3oo feet of the redevelopment project area' is naterial unless the
decision will have no financial effect upon the official's 
real
property interests.
PLanning Cornmissioner Harris owns two pieces of real property
irhich are within 3Oo feet of the redevelopment project area and
Planning Cornmissioner curry owns a personal residence which is
within 3oo feet of the project area. Because of the close
proxi:nity of Planning Cornrnissioners Harris' and curry's real
property to the subject property, there is a presunption that the
iinlncii:. 
effect on the real property wiII be rnaterial. This
presurnption may be rebutted only by a showing that there will 
be
no financial effect on either planning comrnissioners real property
interests. 
(Regulation La102.3(a) (1); PhelDS Advice Letter, No-
A-88-429. )
You have provided us with no facts to indicate that the real
property of Planning Commissioners Harris and curry will not be
financially affected by the decisions concerning the redevelopment
project. 
Absent such information we conclude that Planninq
commissioners Harris and curry real property interests will 
be
materially affected by the decision.
Planning Conmissioner Goodwill's property is 1,0o0 to 1,500
feet fron the project area. Regulatj.on LA7O2- 3(a) (3) provides
that the effect of a decision is material as to real property if:
(3) The real property in which the official
has an interest is located outside a radius of 300
feet and any part of the real property is located
within a radius of 2,500 feet of the boundaries (or
proposed boundaries) of the property rrhich is the
iubject of the decision and the decision will have
a reasonably foreseeable effect of:
(A) Ten thOusand dollars (S■0,000)。r
more on the fair market value of the real
property in which the official has an
interest, or
4 Th" boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the redevelopment
project area are the "boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the decisionr whenever the
decision is a redevelopment decision to designate the survey area,
to nake findings of blight, to select the project area, to adopt
the preliminary plan, to forn a project area comrnittee, to certify
the environrnental document, to adopt the redevelopment plan, to
add territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend
any of the above decisions. (Regulation LA7OZ.3(e).)


(B) WiIl affect the rental value of the
property by S1,OOO or more per 12 month
period.
consistent with the foreseeability discussion in the oqelsbv
opinion, the effect of the redevelopnent project on property
adjacent to the project will be a substantial increase in the
property's value. we cannot definitively determine whether the
lnagnituae of the financial effect on the property interests of
elinning Commissioner GoodwilI caused by the redeveloprnent project
is disqualifying. 
we nust leave this factual determination of
dol.larjeffecl to you and Planning commissioner GoodwiIl r'rithin the
guidelines provided by Regulation 18702.3.
You also stated that Pl'anning Cornmissioner GoodwilI's
daughter owns a piece of property which is Leased for conmercial
use and is located the sane distance from the redevelopment
project area. You stated the daughter is a dependent 9tt-i:-a'
Seclion 87103 provj.des that property or.rned by an official's
imrnediate famj-Iy is a disqualifying economic interest of the
official, 
becauie the official 
would have an "indirect interest"
in the property. "Immediate family" is defined in Section 82029
as the tfficlai'. 
spouse and depenient children.5 Thus, the
property of the planning commissioner's daughter is treated as the
planning comnissioner's property under the Act.
B. Sources of Income
Planning Commissioners curry and Franco also have sources of
income in the project area. Planning commissioner Harris has a
source of incoire witnin 3oo feet of the project area. Planning
Commissioner Goodwill has sources of incone which are business
entities within 1,ooo to 1,500 feet of the redevelopment project
area .
where a source of income is not directl'y before the planning
cornrnission, as is the case here, but nay be indirectly affected by
a planning commission decision, Regulations L8702.2 and 18702'6
appIy. Whether the indirect effect on a business-entitv soqrce of
iirloire is material depends on the financial size of the business
entitV. 
For exanple, if Planning Cornmissioner Goodwill's lessees
are rLlatively snall business entities, Regulati-on 18702.2(q)
Our File No. I-9■
-32■
Page 8
5  A child is considered a l:dependent child:: for purposes of the
Act if the child is under 18 and the elected officia■
 is entitled
to claim the dependent child as such for income tax purposes。
(Tremlett Advice Letter′  No. ■-89-386。
)

Page 9
provides that for such snaII business entities the indirect effect
of a decision is naterial where:
(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,0o0 or tnore; or
(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the anount of S2,50o or nore; or
(3) The decision will result in the increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of S10,000 or more.
consequently, assurning that subdivision (g) is the
appropriate standard, and the redevelopment decisions will affect
the gross revenues of the businesses that Iease Planning
Commissioner Goodwill's property by $ro,00o or nore for a fiscaL
year, or the value of the lessees' assets or liabilities 
by
Slo,ooo or nore, or will result in the lessees incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of 52,500 or more,
Planning Comrnissioner GoodlriII tnay not participate.
where a source of incone is an individual, as appears to be
the case with Planning Conrnissioners Harris and curry, and the
individual is not directly before the planning commission but may
be affected indirectly, Regulation La702.6 provides:
The effect of a decision is naterial as to an
individual r.rho is a source of incorne or gifts to an
offj,cial if any of the following applies:
(a) The decision wiLl affect the
individual's i.ncorne, investments, or other
tangible or intangible assets or liabilities
(other than real property) by $1,000 or more;
or
(b) The decision will affect the
individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered rnaterial under
Section 18702.3 or Section L8702.4.
Thus, Planning Cornrnissioner Harris nust disqualify hirnself if
the source of incone to hin wiII be affected by Sf,000 or if the
source of incorne has a property interest in or near the project
area. Planning Commissioner Curry's source of incorne owns

Page ■0
property in the redevelopment project area. Thus, Planning
conrnissioner curry's source of income is also disqualifying with
respect to the decision in question.
c. 
Business Interests
FinaIly, Planning Comrnissioner Franco's economic interest in
his alarrn business will be disquaJ.ifying if the redeveloPment
decision will affect his business by the dollar amounts set forth
in the appropriate provision of Regulation l'8702.2, as discussed
above .
4. 
riPubl ic Generallv" ExceDtion
Public officials with financial interests that r'riIl be
rnaterially affected by a decision nay participate in the decision
if the efiect on their proPerty is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public genera1J.y. For the "public generalJ"y"
exception to appIy, a decision nust affect the official's
interests in substantiallv the same manner as it lrould affect a
siqnificant seqment of the public. (ReguJ'ation 18703.)
The "public'r consists of the entire jurisdiction of the
agency in iuestion. 
(In re owen (L976) 2 FPPC ops. 77-) This is
s6 belau=e aIl the residents of the jurisdiction are constituents
of the official. 
Thus, the planning commissioners may
participate in the redeveloprnent decisions if the effect on the
ilanninq commissioners financial interests is substantiallY the
SAES .= th" .ffect on a siqnificant secrment of the population of
the city of East PaIo Alto.
According to your facts, the project area covers 180 acres of
the city's 2.5 square niles of area (1,500 acres) - Thus, the area
covered by the pioject is approxiruately 12 percent of the city'
You also itated that of the 3,410 single-farnily residences in the
city, 241 are located in or within 3oo feet of the project area'
Thi3 equates to approximately 7 Percent of the city's single
f arni J.y res idences .
The commission has never adopted a strict arithmetic f ormul'a
for determining what constitutes a significant segnent of the
public. 
However, in order to apply the t'public generallyrl
exceptj-on, the affected population must be large and heterogeneous
in nature. (In re Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC oPs. 62.) while the
population affected appears to be heterogeneous in nature, we do
not bel j,eve that 7 percent of the single family residences in the
city or 12 percent of the area of the city is sufficiently 
large


to invoke the exception.6 Thus, it is unlikeJ.y the "public
generally" exception lrould pernit Planning Commissioners Harris
and Curry to participate in the decision since a relative small
portion of the population in the city would be affected in the
same manner as the planning comrnissioners.
Moreover, you described the area as currently zoned for
single and nulti-family residential and commercial uses- Thus, it
is unlikely that the area constitutes the core of comrnercial
business in the city. 
(see e.q., In re orren (1976) 2 rPPC Ops.
77.) Therefore, the exception woul.d not perrnit cornmissioner
Franco to participate despite his business interest in the project
area. Sirnilarly, it appears that despite the fact that the
businesses inside the project area will not conpete with Planning
conmissioner Goodwill's Iessees, the lessee's will stiIl 
benefit
greatly frorn the redevelopment of the area adjacent to their
6usineis and wiIl be affected differently from other businesses
more distant. 
consequently, in each case, the planning
commissioners' economic interests will be affected in a manner
Cifferent that the rest of the population of East PaIo AIto.
In addition to the traditj'onaI I'public generalLy exception
;.n the Act, ho$rever. Regulation 18703.1 al'so provides a "public
generally" exception for smaIl jurisdictions. 
Regulation 18703'l
lrovides-that u;der certain specified circumstances the effect of
i go.r...,*..,tal decision on a pubJ'ic official's 
princi.pal residence
is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally in
smal.1 jurisdiitions. 
However, the exception is only available
with r6spect to the pub).ic official's 
interest in his or her
principal residence as follows:
(a) The effect of a governnental decision on
the principal residence of a public official 
is not
dist inguishabl.e fron the effect on the public
generatly where aIl of the following conditions are
met:
(1) The public official's 
agency has
jurisdiction over a population of 25,0oo or
Iess, covering a geographic area of ten square
miles or less.
Our Fi■e No. ■-9■-32■
Page ■■
6 Th" t'public generally" exception is an exception to the general
rules requiring disqual i f ication on the basis of an official's
economic interests. 
It is a fundarnental rule of statutory
construction that an exception to a general rule is to be narrouly
construed. (Julius coldman's Eqq citv v. Air PolIution Control
Dept. of Ventura Countv (1981) 116 Ca1.App.3d 741.) As such, the
narrow interpretation of the exception is preferred.


(2) The public official 
is required to
reside within the jurisdiction.
(3) The public official, 
if he or she is
an elected officer has been elected in an at-
large election.
(4) The decision does not have a direct
effect (as Provided in Regulation
L8702.1 (a) (3) ) on the public official's
princiPat residence.
(5) The DrinciDal residence is more than
3oo feet from the boundaries of the DroDertv
which is the subiect of the decision.
(6) The principal residence is located
on a parcel of I'and not tnore than one-quarter
acre in size or, alternatively, a residentiaL
Iot not larqer than 125 percent of the median
residential Iot size for the jurisdiction'
*t*
(c) Even if the above conditions are not met,
the effect of a governmental decision on the
pri.ncipal residence of a public official 
may be
deternined not to be distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally in accordance with
Regulation 18703 or any other regulations of the
Commission not applicable solely to small
j urisdictions.
white the characteristics of your jurisdiction do falL within
the scope of the reguJ.ation, the excePtj-on would not appJ'y to
elannj,ng Commissioners Goodwill and Franco if they are
dj,squatified due to business interests or sources of incorne '
(Rejulation 18703.1(a) .) 
Nor rrould the exception apply to the
."ui p.op..ty interests of planning Comnissioners Harris and Curry
since-lotn own residences within 3oo feet of the project area'
(Regulation 18703. 1(a) (5) . )

section 87101 provides a Iirnited exception where an otherwise
disqualified planning cornmissioner may participate if his
participation is legally required:
Section 87100 does not prevent any public
official 
frorn making or participating in the naking
of a governmental decision to the extent his


Our File No. 1-9■
-32■
Page ■3
participation is legally required for the action or
decision to be made.
secti.on 87101.
This exception has been narrowty interpreted to penlit the
participation of the fewest financiallv interested persons
bossible in anv decision. (fn re Hudson (1978) 4 FPPC oPs. 13;
EifL Ad./ice Lett*, 
No. I-89-160.) consequently, Regulation 18701
provides in pertinent Part:
(a) A public official 
is not IegaIIy required
to make or to particiPate in the naking of a
governmental decision within the meaning of
6overnment code section 871'01 unless there exists
no alternative source of decision consistent with
the purposes and terms of the statute authorizing
the decision.
,**
(c) This regulation shall' be construed
narrowly, and sha 1l :
(1) Not be construed to Permit an
officiat, 
nho is otherwise disqualified under
Governnent Code Section 8710o, to vote to
break a tie.
(2) Not be construed to allow a nember
of any public agency, who is otherwise
disqull.itied under Governnent code Section
87100, to vote if a quorum can be convened of
other members of the agency !,ho are not
disqualified under Governrnent code section
87100, whether or not such other members are
actuatly present at the tirne of the
disqualification.
Thus, where onLy a single menber is needed to make a quorum,
only one disqualified rnernber is permitted to participate in the
decision. 
1s-kousen Advico Letter, No. A-88-152; Martin Advice
Letter, No. I-88-375.) This is because 'i[T]he purposes of the Act
are best served by a rule which mininizes participation in
government decisions by officials with a conf 1i'ct of interest.rr
(In re Hudson, supra. )
You have asked nhether various decisions concernj.ng the
redevelopment project nay be treated as separate decisions; or,

personal financiaL interests or as to the proposed decision) which

However an agency is not required to repeatedly reselect the

6. Seqmentation of Decis ion
You stated that the planning cornmission would be confronted
with a series of decisions concerning the redevelopnent project in
the near future, including the consideration of an environnental'
irnpact report, general PIan anendment, specific pJ'an, rezoning
arnendment and redeveloprnent pIan. In addition, if the
redevelopment project is approved, the planning conmission.will be
required to coirsider a planned use deveJ.opment permit application,
tentative tract nap and development agreement. For purposes of
this letter ere are assuming that the decisions are project-wide
decisions.
It $/ould appear fron your facts, however, that as the
redevelopnent decisions move forsard there may be sone other
decision! r.rhich wiIl not independently affect some of the planning
commissioners' various interests and may be severable from the
rnajor decisions for which the planning conmissioners were
disquatified. 
Thus. while the conclusions in this letter apply to
rnos€ of the najor redevelopment decisions, a conflict of interest
nay not exist for a specific planning connissioner with respect to
future separable decisions. Therefore lre are providing a general
discussion of segmentation of decisions for your information.
Under many circumstances a series of decisions rnay be too
interrelated to be considered separately. (MiIler Advice Letter,
No. A-82-119.) For exampJ,e, if it is deterrnined that an official
has a conflict of interest as to decisions concerning a specific
project because of the project's i:npact on an economic interest,
the official 
may be sinilarly disqualified as to decisions
concerning the financing of that project. 
The reason is that the
decisions concerning the financing of the project could in fact
alter the previous decisi.on for vhich the officiaL was
disqualified. 
(Nord Advice Letter, No. A-82-038.)


conversely, where the decisions are separable, such as
small.er projects in a larger project, and none affects the
decisioni oi trre other projects, each project may be analyzed
separately to determine if the officiaL has a conflict of
interest. 
(Kilian Advice Letter, No. A-89-522. )
As stated above, it would appear from your facts that as the
redevelopnent decisions move forward they may be separable. rf
indeed tire decisions are separable and a pJ.anning commissioner
deternines he has a conflict of interest with respect to sone, but
not all. the decisions, the foJ'lowing procedure should be folloued
to pernit the planning com:nissioner to participate:
(1) The decisions for shich the planning comrnissioner. has a
disqualifying f j.nancial interest should be segregated frorn the
other decisions.
(2) The decisions from which the planning commissioner is
aisqualified shoul.d be considered first, 
and a final decision
."a6hed by the planning cornmission without the planning
commissioner participating in any way.
(3) once a decision has been made on the projects for which
the pianning commissioner has a disqualj.fying financial interest,
he rnly participate in the deliberations regarding the. other
issuei,- so long as those deliberations do not result in a
reopening or in any uay affect the decisions fron which he was
disguaLiiied. 
(Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343.)
we do not have sufficient inforrnation on each deci.sion to
come before the pJ.anning comrnission to advise on segmentation'

to a specific decision.

sections.conclusion:
S
1. East PaIo AIto Planning commissioners nay not participate
in any decisions which r.riII have a foreseeable and material affect
on any of their econonic interests.
2. The ttpublic generally'r exception vill not apply unless
the decisions wiII affect a significant segment of East Palo Alto
in the sane nanner as the decisions will affect the interests of
the planning conmissioners. This does not appear to be the case
here .

disqualifications, 
a disqualified planning commissioner nay be
tegitty required to participate despite such disqualification. 
A
planning co:nnissioner may participate if he or she is selected at
iandon io achieve a quorum. If necessary, more than one planning
conmissioner may be selected at random to achieve the quorum.
once selected, the same pJ.anning conmissioner or
conmissioners may particiPate in all interrelated decisions.
other disqualified planning commissioners remain disqualified.
Should the commissioner chosen be unable to participate for sone
other reason, other planning conmissioners may not be substituted
for the cornmissioner chosen randomly to achieve the quorum. If
the subject matter of the decision is unchanged as are the
disqualifying interests, there is no basis to repeat the randon
selection process.

sections.analysis:
The Political Reforn Act (the trActrr), was enacted by the
people of the State of California by initiative 
in 1974' The
purpose for the conf I ict-o f- interest provisions of the Act was to
ensure that public officials, 
whether elected or appointed, would
perf orrn their duties in an impartial manner, free fron bias caused
by their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
In furtherance of this goal, section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any publ j,c officiat 
frorn naking, participating in
naking, or otherwise using his or her official 
position to
influence a governnental decision in which the official 
has a
financial interest. 
A "public official" 
is defined in Section
82048 and Regulation L8700 as every natural person who is a
menber, officer, 
ernployee, or consultant of a state or local
government agency. This definition r.rould include nernbers of the
East PaIo Alto Planning Comnission.


Our File No. I-9■
-32■
1. Economic Interests
Section 87103 specifies that an official 
has a financial
interest within the neaning of Section 8710o if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a Daterial financial
effect, distinguishable fron the effect on the public generally,
on the official 
or a roenber of his or her inmediate family or on:
(a) Any business entity in nhich the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investnent lrorth
one thousand dollars ($1,ooo1 or more.
(b) Any real property in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars (S1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of incone, other than gifts
and other than loans by a connercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terns available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating tlro hundred fifty
dollars (S25o) or nore in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 nonths prior to the time lrhen the
decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, Partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any Position of management.

Public officials with financial interests that r'riIl be
rnaterially affected by a decision nay participate in the decision
if the efiect on their proPerty is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public genera1J.y. For the "public generalJ"y"
exception to appIy, a decision nust affect the official's
interests in substantiallv the same manner as it lrould affect a
siqnificant seqment of the public. (ReguJ'ation 18703.)
The "public'r consists of the entire jurisdiction of the
agency in iuestion. 
(In re owen (L976) 2 FPPC ops. 77-) This is
s6 belau=e aIl the residents of the jurisdiction are constituents
of the official. 
Thus, the planning commissioners may
participate in the redeveloprnent decisions if the effect on the
ilanninq commissioners financial interests is substantiallY the
SAES .= th" .ffect on a siqnificant secrment of the population of
the city of East PaIo Alto.
According to your facts, the project area covers 180 acres of

for determining what constitutes a significant segnent of the
public. 
However, in order to apply the t'public generallyrl
exceptj-on, the affected population must be large and heterogeneous
in nature. (In re Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC oPs. 62.) while the
population affected appears to be heterogeneous in nature, we do
not bel j,eve that 7 percent of the single family residences in the
city or 12 percent of the area of the city is sufficiently 
large


to invoke the exception.6 Thus, it is unlikeJ.y the "public
generally" exception lrould pernit Planning Commissioners Harris

77.) Therefore, the exception woul.d not perrnit cornmissioner

greatly frorn the redevelopment of the area adjacent to their

more distant. 
consequently, in each case, the planning
commissioners' economic interests will be affected in a manner
Cifferent that the rest of the population of East PaIo AIto.

;.n the Act, ho$rever. Regulation 18703.1 al'so provides a "public
generally" exception for smaIl jurisdictions.

lrovides-that u;der certain specified circumstances the effect of

is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally in

miles or less.

6 Th" t'public generally" exception is an exception to the general
rules requiring disqual i f ication on the basis of an official's
economic interests. 
It is a fundarnental rule of statutory
construction that an exception to a general rule is to be narrouly
construed. (Julius coldman's Eqq citv v. Air PolIution Control

section 87101 provides a Iirnited exception where an otherwise

This exception has been narrowty interpreted to penlit the

(a) A public official

(c) This regulation shall' be construed

1s-kousen Advico Letter, No. A-88-152; Martin Advice

6. Seqmentation of Decis ion
You stated that the planning cornmission would be confronted
with a series of decisions concerning the redevelopnent project in
the near future, including the consideration of an environnental'
irnpact report, general PIan anendment, specific pJ'an, rezoning

redevelopment project is approved, the planning conmission.will be
required to coirsider a planned use deveJ.opment permit application,
tentative tract nap and development agreement. For purposes of
this letter ere are assuming that the decisions are project-wide
decisions.
It $/ould appear fron your facts, however, that as the
redevelopnent decisions move forsard there may be sone other
decision! r.rhich wiIl not independently affect some of the planning
commissioners' various interests and may be severable from the
rnajor decisions for which the planning conmissioners were
disquatified. 
Thus. while the conclusions in this letter apply to
rnos€ of the najor redevelopment decisions, a conflict of interest
nay not exist for a specific planning connissioner with respect to
future separable decisions. Therefore lre are providing a general
discussion of segmentation of decisions for your information.
Under many circumstances a series of decisions rnay be too
interrelated to be considered separately. (MiIler Advice Letter,
No. A-82-119.) For exampJ,e, if it is deterrnined that an official
has a conflict of interest as to decisions concerning a specific

concerning the financing of that project.

disqualified. 
(Nord Advice Letter, No. A-82-038.)


conversely, where the decisions are separable, such as
small.er projects in a larger project, and none affects the
decisioni oi trre other projects, each project may be analyzed
separately to determine if the officiaL has a conflict of
interest. 
(Kilian Advice Letter, No. A-89-522. )
As stated above, it would appear from your facts that as the
redevelopnent decisions move forward they may be separable. rf
indeed tire decisions are separable and a pJ.anning commissioner
deternines he has a conflict of interest with respect to sone, but
not all. the decisions, the foJ'lowing procedure should be folloued
to pernit the planning com:nissioner to participate:
(1) The decisions for shich the planning comrnissioner. has a

other decisions.
(2) The decisions from which the planning commissioner is
aisqualified shoul.d be considered first, 
and a final decision
."a6hed by the planning cornmission without the planning
commissioner participating in any way.

he rnly participate in the deliberations regarding the. other
issuei,- so long as those deliberations do not result in a
reopening or in any uay affect the decisions fron which he was
disguaLiiied. 
(Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343.)
we do not have sufficient inforrnation on each deci.sion to

to a specific decision.

QUESTION: 1. May East PaIo Alto Planning Commissioners participate in
decisions concerning the Gateway RedeveloPnent Project if the
decisions nay directly and indirectly affect their real property
i nterests ?

commission
reguJ.ations appear at 2 california code of Regulations Section

Division 5 of the California code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the

83114; Regul,ation 18329 (c) (3). )
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-O8OZ . (916) 322-5660

2. wilI the decisions on the redevelopment project affect
the public Aenerally in the sane Banner as the pJ.anning
connissioners pursuant to the exceptions in the Act?
3. If a rnajority of planning cornmissioners are disqualified
due to financiaL interests which will be affected by the decision,
tnay sorne of the officials 
be perroitted to participate throughout
the series of decisions concerning the redevelopment area?

CONCLUSION: S
1. East PaIo AIto Planning commissioners nay not participate
in any decisions which r.riII have a foreseeable and material affect
on any of their econonic interests.
2. The ttpublic generally'r exception vill not apply unless
the decisions wiII affect a significant segment of East Palo Alto
in the sane nanner as the decisions will affect the interests of
the planning conmissioners. This does not appear to be the case
here .
3. Where a quorun cannot be achieved due to
disqualifications, 
a disqualified planning commissioner nay be
tegitty required to participate despite such disqualification. 
A
planning co:nnissioner may participate if he or she is selected at
iandon io achieve a quorum. If necessary, more than one planning
conmissioner may be selected at random to achieve the quorum.
once selected, the same pJ.anning conmissioner or
conmissioners may particiPate in all interrelated decisions.
other disqualified planning commissioners remain disqualified.
Should the commissioner chosen be unable to participate for sone
other reason, other planning conmissioners may not be substituted
for the cornmissioner chosen randomly to achieve the quorum. If
the subject matter of the decision is unchanged as are the
disqualifying interests, there is no basis to repeat the randon
selection process.
